revision.text: {{出典の明記|date=2016年8月22日 (月) 15:30}} 玉依御前（たまよりごぜん）は、[[佐伯田公]]（さえき の たぎみ）の妻で、[[空海]]の母親である。なお、空海は次男で、長男に[[佐伯鈴伎麻呂]]がいる。奈良時代から平安時代初期ごろにかけての人物。 <gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|仏母院の母公堂 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ø.jpg|慈尊院の拝堂 </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2016年8月22日 (月) 15:30}} '''玉依御前'''（たまよりごぜん）は、[[佐伯田公]]（さえき の たぎみ）の妻で、[[空海]]（弘法大師）の母親である。なお、空海は次男で、長男に[[佐伯鈴伎麻呂]]がいる。奈良時代から平安時代初期ごろにかけての人物。 <gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|仏母院の母公堂 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ø.jpg|慈尊院の拝堂 </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2016年8月22日 (月) 15:30}} '''玉依御前'''（たまよりごぜん）は、[[佐伯田公]]（さえき の たぎみ）の妻で、[[空海]]（弘法大師）の母親である。なお、空海は次男で、長男に[[佐伯鈴伎麻呂]]がいる。奈良時代から平安時代初期ごろにかけての人物。 <gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|仏母院の母公堂 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ø.jpg|慈尊院の拝堂 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|善通寺御影堂の前 </gallery>

'''玉依御前'''（たまよりごぜん）は、[[佐伯田公]]（さえき の たぎみ）の妻で、[[空海]]（弘法大師）の母親である。なお、空海は次男で、長男に[[佐伯鈴伎麻呂]]がいる。奈良時代から平安時代初期ごろにかけての人物。 <gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|仏母院の母公堂 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ø.jpg|慈尊院の拝堂 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|善通寺御影堂の前 </gallery> {{Buddhism-stub}}

'''玉依御前'''（たまよりごぜん）は、[[佐伯田公]]（さえき の たぎみ）の妻で、[[空海]]（弘法大師）の母親である。なお、空海は次男で、長男に[[佐伯鈴伎麻呂]]がいる。奈良時代から平安時代初期ごろにかけての人物。 == 概要 == 一説では、玉依御前は、阿刀[[宿禰]]（あとのすくね）の娘で、兄弟には[[伊予親王]]の侍講を務めた阿刀大足（あとのおおたり）がいた有力な家系であった。実家は[[善通寺]]から数ｋｍの現在の[[仏母院]]がある位置にあったとされ、近くにあった熊手八幡宮に祈願し空海を懐妊したと伝えられている。仏母院は八幡山三角寺仏母院と云い、実家のあった御住屋敷（みすみやしき）に、空海が唐から帰国して三角寺を建立し、熊手八幡宮の別当寺になったと云われている。<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|仏母院の母公堂 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ø.jpg|慈尊院の拝堂 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|善通寺御影堂の前 </gallery> {{Buddhism-stub}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''玉依御前'''（たまよりごぜん）は、[[奈良時代]]から[[平安時代]]の女性。[[佐伯善通]]の正室。[[空海]]の生母。なお、空海は次男で、長男に[[佐伯鈴伎麻呂]]がいる。
名は「阿古屋（あこや）御前」との説もあるが<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>、正確なところは不詳。生誕年も同様に不明で、没年も曼荼羅寺の縁起の「空海が唐から帰朝した翌年の大同2年（807年）に当寺で亡き母の菩提を弔った」という記述や、他にも承和2年（835年）2月5日に亡くなったなど、諸説ある。
== 概要 == 一説では、玉依御前は、[[安斗智徳|阿刀宿禰]]（あとのすくね）の娘で、兄弟には[[伊予親王]]の侍講を務めた[[阿刀大足]]（あとのおおたり）がいた有力な家系であった。実家は[[善通寺]]から数ｋｍの現在の[[仏母院]]がある位置にあったとされ、近くにあった[[熊手八幡宮]]に祈願し空海を懐妊したと伝えられている。
仏母院は八幡山三角寺仏母院と云い、実家のあった御住屋敷（みすみやしき）に、空海が唐から帰国して三角寺を建立し、熊手八幡宮の別当寺になったと云われている<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>。空海のヘソの緒を納めた胞衣塚（えなづか）や空海が幼少のおり作った泥仏が現存している。
玉依御前が空海を産んだ他の場所の説として、仏母院から西へ約2kmの[[海岸寺]]御影堂の前に産井があって、ここだという説がある。また、善通寺御影堂の横にも産井があり、ここだという説がある。また、学術的には阿刀氏の一族は摂津や河内などの畿内に居住していたとされ、空海は畿内で生まれ育ったという説もある。
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|玉依御前像 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|弥谷寺の獅子之岩屋 File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|仏母院の母公堂 File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|慈尊院の拝堂 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|善通寺御影堂の前 File:Butimoin 01.jpg|仏母院の産井 File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|海岸寺の産井 File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|善通寺の産井 </gallery>
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
{{Buddhism-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''玉依御前'''（たまよりごぜん、生年不明 - [[835年]]（[[承和 (日本)|承和]]2年））は、[[奈良時代]]から[[平安時代]]の女性。[[佐伯田公]]の正室。[[空海]]の生母。なお、空海は次男で、長男に[[佐伯鈴伎麻呂]]がいる。

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

[[Category:Ato clan]] [[Category:Saeki clan]] [[Category:Kūkai]] [[Category:Women of the Nara period]] [[Category:Women of the Heian period]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:835 deaths]] [[Category:New pages]][[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tamayori Gozen]][[Category:pp]] [[ja:玉依御前]]

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
{{Buddhism-stub}}

Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.

The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. {{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

[[ja:玉依御前]]

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.

== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
{{Buddhism-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
{{Buddhism-stub}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王|1=Iyo Shinnō}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王|1=Iyo Shinnō}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2021st year 4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3rd year （836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王|1=Iyo Shinnō}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012nd year 12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.

{{出典の明記|date=2021st year 4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3rd year （836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王|1=Iyo Shinnō}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012nd year 12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3rd year （836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. == Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳|12=draft}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足|12=draft}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王|1=Iyo Shinnō|12=draft}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院|12=draft}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺|12=draft}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮|12=draft}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012nd year 12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺|12=draft}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

28 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:玉依御前 up to rev 103088532
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''玉依御前'''（たまよりごぜん、生年不明 - [[835年]]（[[承和 (日本)|承和]]2年））は、[[奈良時代]]から[[平安時代]]の女性。[[佐伯田公]]の正室。[[空海]]の生母。なお、空海は次男で、長男に[[佐伯鈴伎麻呂]]がいる。
名は「阿古屋（あこや）御前」との説もあるが<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>、正確なところは不詳。生誕年も同様に不明で、没年も曼荼羅寺の縁起の「空海が唐から帰朝した翌年の大同2年（807年）に当寺で亡き母の菩提を弔った」という記述や、他にも承和2年（835年）2月5日に亡くなったなど、諸説ある。
== 生涯 == 一説では玉依御前は、[[安斗智徳|阿刀宿禰]]（あとのすくね）の娘で、兄弟には[[伊予親王]]の侍講を務めた[[阿刀大足]]（あとのおおたり）がいた有力な家系であった。実家は[[善通寺]]から少し離れた場所にある[[仏母院]]がある位置にあったとされ、近くにあった[[熊手八幡宮]]に祈願し空海を懐妊したと伝えられている。
仏母院は八幡山三角寺仏母院と云い、実家のあった御住屋敷（みすみやしき）に、空海が唐から帰国して三角寺を建立し、熊手八幡宮の別当寺になったと云われている<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著2012年12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>。空海のヘソの緒を納めた胞衣塚（えなづか）や空海が幼少のおり作った泥仏が現存している。
玉依御前が空海を産んだ他の場所の説として、仏母院から西へ約2kmの[[海岸寺 (香川県多度津町)|海岸寺]]御影堂の前に産井があって、ここだという説がある。また、善通寺御影堂の横にも産井があり、ここだという説がある。また、学術的には阿刀氏の一族は摂津や河内などの畿内に居住していたとされ、空海は畿内で生まれ育ったという説もある。
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|玉依御前像 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|弥谷寺の獅子之岩屋 File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|仏母院の母公堂 File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|慈尊院の拝堂 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|善通寺御影堂の前 File:Butimoin 01.jpg|仏母院の産井 File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|海岸寺の産井 File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|善通寺の産井 </gallery>

{{出典の明記|date=2021年4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''玉依御前'''（たまよりごぜん、生年不明 - [[835年]]（[[承和 (日本)|承和]]2年））は、[[奈良時代]]から[[平安時代]]の女性。[[佐伯田公]]の正室。[[空海]]の生母。なお、空海は次男で、長男に[[佐伯鈴伎麻呂]]がいる。
名は「阿古屋（あこや）御前」との説もあるが<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3年（836年）2月紀。</ref>、正確なところは不詳。生誕年も同様に不明で、没年も曼荼羅寺の縁起の「空海が唐から帰朝した翌年の大同2年（807年）に当寺で亡き母の菩提を弔った」という記述や、他にも承和2年（835年）2月5日に亡くなったなど、諸説ある。
== 生涯 == 一説では玉依御前は、[[安斗智徳|阿刀宿禰]]（あとのすくね）の娘で、兄弟には[[伊予親王]]の侍講を務めた[[阿刀大足]]（あとのおおたり）がいた有力な家系であった。実家は[[善通寺]]から少し離れた場所にある[[仏母院]]がある位置にあったとされ、近くにあった[[熊手八幡宮]]に祈願し空海を懐妊したと伝えられている。

玉依御前が空海を産んだ他の場所の説として、仏母院から西へ約2kmの[[海岸寺 (香川県多度津町)|海岸寺]]御影堂の前に産井があって、ここだという説がある。また、善通寺御影堂の横にも産井があり、ここだという説がある。また、学術的には阿刀氏の一族は摂津や河内などの畿内に居住していたとされ、空海は畿内で生まれ育ったという説もある。
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|玉依御前像 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|弥谷寺の獅子之岩屋 File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|仏母院の母公堂 File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|慈尊院の拝堂 File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|善通寺御影堂の前 File:Butimoin 01.jpg|仏母院の産井 File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|海岸寺の産井 File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|善通寺の産井 </gallery>

Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Saeki clan]]
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2021st year 4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - {{ill|835|ja|承和 (日本)|lt=Shōwa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/835|qq=}} 2) was a woman from the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara period|qq=}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|qq=}}, and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki no Suzukimaro|qq=}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3rd year （836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ato no Sukune|qq=}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ato no Ōtari|qq=}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王|1=Iyo Shinnō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iyo|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo Shinnō|qq=}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumoin|qq=}}, a short distance from {{ill|Zentsū-ji|ja|善通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsū-ji|qq=}}. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumade Hachiman Shrine|qq=}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012nd year 12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaigan-ji Temple|qq=}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>
== Notes ==

She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki no Suzukimaro|qq=}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3rd year （836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).

Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumoin|qq=}}, a short distance from [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]]. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumade Hachiman Shrine|qq=}} before conceiving Kūkai.

Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaigan-ji Temple|qq=}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2021st year 4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - [[:en:Jōwa (Heian period)|Shōwa]] 2) was a woman from the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki no Suzukimaro|qq=}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3rd year （836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ato no Sukune|qq=}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ato no Ōtari|qq=}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王|1=Iyo Shinnō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iyo|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo Shinnō|qq=}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumoin|qq=}}, a short distance from [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]]. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumade Hachiman Shrine|qq=}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012nd year 12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaigan-ji Temple|qq=}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>
== Notes ==

[[ja:玉依御前]]
<!--Tamayori Gozen--><!--Tamayori Gozen--><!--Tamayori Gozen--><!--Tamayori Gozen--><!--Tamayori Gozen--><!--Tamayori Gozen--><!--Tamayori Gozen--><!--Tamayori Gozen--> [[Category:Saeki clan]] [[Category:Women of the Nara period]] [[Category:Women of the Heian period]] [[Category:Ato clan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Tamayori Gozen}} {{wikidata link|Q27917346}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tamayori Gozen]][[ja:玉依御前]] {{Infobox person|death_date={{death date|0835|2|5}}|gender={{ill|female|qid=Q6581072}}|children={{ill|Kūkai|qid=Q222227}}; {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|qid=Q11382909}}|father={{ill|Q11450750|qid=Q11450750}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q27917346]] Wikidata name: Tamayorigozen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2021st year 4月13日 (月) 21:14 (UTC)}} '''Tamayori Gozen''' (玉依御前, 生年不明 - [[:en:Jōwa (Heian period)|Shōwa]] 2) was a woman from the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. She was the wife of Saeki no Ata, the mother of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], and the second son, while her eldest son was {{ill|Saeki no Suzukimaro|ja|佐伯鈴伎麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki no Suzukimaro|qq=|1=Saeki no Suzukimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11382909}}.
Her name is also referred to as "Akoya Gozen" (阿古屋御前) in some accounts<ref>『続日本後紀』承和3rd year （836年）2月紀。</ref>, but the exact details remain unknown. Her birth year is uncertain, and there are multiple theories about her death year, such as the record in the Mandaraji temple’s history stating that Kūkai prayed for his deceased mother’s soul in Daidō 2 (807), the year following his return from Tang China, or that she passed away on February 5, Shōwa 2 (835).
== Life == One theory suggests that Tamayori Gozen was the daughter of {{ill|Ato no Sukune|ja|安斗智徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ato no Sukune|qq=}}, with a brother named {{ill|Ato no Ōtari|ja|阿刀大足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ato no Ōtari|qq=}}, who served as a tutor to {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王|1=Iyo Shinnō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iyo|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo Shinnō|qq=}}. Her family is believed to have been influential and resided near the current location of {{ill|Butsumoin|ja|仏母院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsumoin|qq=}}, a short distance from [[:en:Zentsū-ji|Zentsū-ji]]. It is said that she prayed at {{ill|Kumade Hachiman Shrine|ja|熊手八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumade Hachiman Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} before conceiving Kūkai.
The Butsumoin, also known as Hachiman-san Sankaku-ji Butsumoin, was said to have been established by Kūkai upon his return from Tang China. The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012nd year 12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaigan-ji Temple|qq=|1=Kaigan-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558882}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
<gallery> File:Tamayoriɡozen.jpg|Statue of Tamayori Gozen File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɾ.jpg|Shishi-no-Iwaya at Mitani-dera File:Tamayoriɡozenœɨ.jpg|Mother's Hall at Butsumoin File:Tamayorigozen02.jpg|Haiden at Jison-in File:Tamayoriɡozen0ɜ.jpg|Front of the Mieidō at Zentsū-ji File:Butimoin 01.jpg|Birthing well at Butsumoin File:Kaiɡanziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Kaigan-ji File:Zentuziøɨ.jpg|Birthing well at Zentsū-ji </gallery>

The temple became the head temple of Kumade Hachiman Shrine<ref>四国「弘法大師の霊跡」巡り 224ページ 川崎一洋・著 2012nd year 12月18日発行 セルバ出版を参照</ref>. Artifacts such as Kūkai’s umbilical cord stored in the "Enazuka" mound and a mud Buddha sculpted by Kūkai as a child still exist.
Another location proposed as her birthplace is near the {{ill|Kaigan-ji Temple|ja|海岸寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaigan-ji Temple|1=Kaigan-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558882}} Mieidō Hall, where a birthing well is located about 2 km west of Butsumoin. Other claims suggest a birthing well near the Mieidō Hall at Zentsū-ji. Academic perspectives propose that the Ato clan lived in the Kinai region, such as Settsu or Kawachi, and that Kūkai was born and raised in Kinai.
